And we're making money, all the money that's being spent running the campaign. Now, there's nothing illegal about it. There's no law against it other than that the charges have to be reasonable and customary. So the print shop can't charge $50 for something that should cost five, but they can charge reasonable and customary fees with reasonable profits in it. So if the candidate gets a self-published book done and his brother-in-law owns the book printing company, as long as the charge is reasonable and customary, the profit goes to the brother-in-law, who he had to support anyway, so better he does it with public dollars rather than private dollars, right? So now, if that was all disclosed, would people feel differently about, yeah, but it doesn't have to be disclosed. So it's deception by omission, by non-statement. Everybody draws their lines about all this stuff situationally. Everybody's got a moving line. Anybody in here that says they got a permanent line or anybody who tells you they got a permanent line, they don't. You make these kind of decisions as you go. And we all do, right, make these kind of decisions as we go, right? So Bill tells you they decided to sell the CDs last night and printed up the order form in the dead of night because everybody would want it, right? Now that happens probably to be absolutely true. There was probably no advanced thought to this, but would it matter if there was? Well, you got to decide that for yourself from both sides of that equation. Would it matter if that was a gambit? Would it matter? Doesn't really. It might matter in effectiveness, but does it matter to the end user who's getting the CDs? It's like a tele-seminar that is replayed and pretended to be live, and the people believe it is live because they more show up that way. But it's not live. So is that deceptive? Obviously it's deceptive. Now it can be blatantly deceptive. It can be deceptive by implication and assumption. It can be deceptive by omission, but it's deceptive. What's the end result? Well, the person on the line has the exact same experience they would have as if the tele-seminar was live. If they think it's live and it sounds live and it behaves live and people get to ask questions and they're pushing the star button and they just don't get through, they have entirely the same experience as if you did it live. So is there any harm? No. If they buy from it, were they cheated or defrauded or any of that in some way? No. Because if they had bought from it live, the end result's the same. They pay the same price, they get the same stuff, they get the same value out of it, whatever value it is that they go get out of it. So in theory, the only truly non-deceptive thing to do would be to fully disclose this is not a live tele- somebody come on at the beginning. This is not a live tele-seminar. Everybody should know this is a pre-recorded tele-seminar. It was pre-recorded 322 years ago. The guy's dead. You know, et cetera. And obviously you could hear the hangups all across the country. Click, click, click, click, click, click, click, because everybody left, right? So just a little bit about that. The second thing I would say is that it is important to understand, everything that is talked about here has to be applied differently in different situations and you have to give some careful thought and not just run off, bull in China shop, especially if you got something that's working, and just change it all because you sat in a seminar for two days and you heard an example, or you really gotta think these things through. For example, there's a difference between local versus national, there's a difference between niche versus non-niche. In the case of the IBAs who are in the room, who are running local chapters, that's really sort of a catcher, a point of entry device, and your behavior as a chapter president and running local feel good meetings has to be a little different than your behavior running the coaching meeting where people ascend and are paying serious money and are behind serious doors. So, this is just sort of a basis for thought about now, what should I do in my situation with my people, with my circumstances? It is not like Moses coming down the hill with the 11 commandments and everybody is supposed to follow them literally, right? Everything has to be considered in the context of your particular market, your particular clientele, your particular fee structure, and so forth. With all of that said, let's sort of pick up where we left off.
